content.text: golden path for agent workflow applications

is the platform-managed, paved road that encodes every architectural decision your team would otherwise spend weeks debating and revisiting — LLM model selection, orchestration topology, tool contract shape, memory tier layout, human-approval wiring, observability hooks, and Kubernetes deployment manifests. Running
produces a working scaffold that already satisfies these decisions. The only job left for the application engineer is filling in the domain-specific logic.
is a pre-configured, opinionated application archetype that a platform engineering team maintains on behalf of product teams. For agentic applications the golden path bundles:
— model name, system-prompt template, max_steps, timeout, and LLM connection configuration.
— a pointer list into the platform tool registry that declares exactly which OpenAPI-backed functions the agent can invoke, their required permission scopes, and JSON Schema constraints.
— a Pydantic model (or TypedDict) that represents the complete mutable state threaded through the ReAct loop.
— a LangGraph StateGraph, CrewAI Crew definition, or Temporal workflow file depending on which orchestrator the platform standardises on.
— Deployment, HPA, ConfigMap, and ServiceAccount with least-privilege annotations wired to the platform's IRSA or Workload Identity setup.
— OpenTelemetry instrumentation pre-wired to the platform's collector endpoint, emitting spans per LLM call, tool call latency, and HITL event.

Building a capable agent from scratch is straightforward; building one that is correct and safe enough to run autonomously in production is far harder. Left to decide independently, each team makes different choices — some agents run unconstrained loops, others have no audit trail, most lack injection defenses. The golden path eliminates these inconsistencies at the source.
Teams spend time on domain logic, not infrastructure decisions that were already made correctly once.
Security, compliance, and observability requirements are satisfied by default — not bolted on after a security review.
Upgrades to the underlying LLM model, orchestrator version, or tool SDK are applied centrally and propagated to all apps on the path.
Incident response is faster because every agent shares the same telemetry structure and audit log format.
— identical HITL and audit patterns across dozens of agent apps reduce cognitive load for on-call engineers.
— a new agent can reach staging in hours rather than weeks because infrastructure decisions ship with the scaffold.
— max_steps guards, tool permission scopes, output sanitization, and prompt-injection defenses are pre-configured.
— token usage, p99 tool latency, and approval rate KPIs are available from day one without custom instrumentation.
— platform updates (model versions, SDK patches) flow to all golden-path apps through a controlled upgrade process.
— teams that need non-standard orchestration patterns (e.g., nested multi-agent topologies with cross-team tool sharing) hit the scaffold's edges and must either extend it or diverge from the path.
— the scaffold assumes the platform's tool registry, secret manager, and OTEL endpoint are available; running outside the platform requires manual adaptation.
— the path standardises one orchestrator per tier (e.g., LangGraph for stateless, Temporal for long-running). Teams with strong opposing preferences will push back.
— engineers used to ad-hoc agent scripts must understand the scaffold conventions (state schema shape, graph node naming, YAML contract) before being productive.
Every request through a golden-path agent follows the same lifecycle:
The orchestrator initialises the typed state object from the incoming payload.
The ReAct loop sends the current state (system prompt + conversation history + tool schemas) to the LLM and receives either a final answer or a tool_use block.

The tool dispatcher validates the tool call against the JSON Schema contract in the tool manifest, checks that the tool is in scope for this agent's permission set, executes the OpenAPI-backed function, and appends the result to state.

The memory manager checks context window budget. If messages exceed the configured token ceiling it summarises older turns using a lighter model and evicts them, writing the summary to the working memory slot in Redis.

If the tool call or planned action triggers a HITL rule (e.g., a write operation above a cost threshold, or a destructive action flag), the agent pauses and emits a webhook or Temporal signal to an approver. Execution resumes only when the approval callback arrives.
Loop repeats until the LLM returns a final answer or max_steps is reached. Every step emits an OpenTelemetry span. Every HITL event is written to the immutable audit log.

attrs.alt: Agent Workflow Golden Path architecture: Platform Scaffold generates agent definition, which drives the Agent Core (ReAct loop, tool dispatch, memory manager, HITL gate) that communicates bidirectionally with LLM Provider, Tool Registry, Memory Layers, and Human Approver; Orchestration (LangGraph/CrewAI/Temporal), Observability, and Audit Trail connect from the right.

content.text: — any workflow that calls external APIs, databases, or shell commands on behalf of the LLM. The tool registry and permission model are essential for these.
— agents that search, fetch, synthesise, and write output over several turns benefit from the memory manager's context budget enforcement.
— any agent authorised to send emails, modify infrastructure, or initiate transactions must use the HITL gate wiring that ships with the path.
— when a single agent run might span minutes to hours (e.g., multi-document processing, overnight research), use the Temporal orchestration variant for durable state.

Do not use the golden path when you are exploring capabilities in a notebook, building a demo that will never run in production, or prototyping a custom multi-agent topology that genuinely requires escaping the scaffold's constraints.

# Platform CLI generates the full project skeleton
platform new agent-app --name market-monitor \
  --orchestrator langgraph \
  --tier stateless

# Generated layout:
# market-monitor/
#   agent.yaml            -- agent definition
#   tool-manifest.json    -- tool declarations
#   state_schema.py       -- typed state
#   graph.py              -- LangGraph graph definition
#   k8s/                  -- Deployment, HPA, ConfigMap
#   otel_config.yaml      -- collector endpoint wiring

# agent.yaml
name: market-monitor
model: claude-opus-4-5
max_steps: 20
timeout_seconds: 300
system_prompt: |
  You are a market intelligence agent. Use the available tools to
  research companies, fetch financial data, and produce structured reports.
  Never speculate about future stock prices. Cite every data point.
tool_manifest: ./tool-manifest.json
hitl:
  triggers:
    - action: send_email
      condition: always
    - action: write_database
      condition: row_count > 100
memory:
  context_budget_tokens: 80000
  working_memory: redis://platform-cache:6379/0
  long_term: qdrant://platform-vectors:6333/market-monitor

# graph.py
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from state_schema import AgentState
from platform_sdk.tools import load_tool_registry
from platform_sdk.llm import get_llm
from platform_sdk.hitl import hitl_gate
from platform_sdk.memory import MemoryManager

llm = get_llm()                          # reads model from agent.yaml
tools = load_tool_registry()             # loads tool-manifest.json
memory = MemoryManager()                 # reads memory config from agent.yaml

def reason(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Call the LLM with current state; get next action or final answer."""
    messages = memory.trim_to_budget(state["messages"])
    response = llm.invoke(messages, tools=tools)
    return {**state, "messages": state["messages"] + [response]}

def dispatch(state: AgentState) -> AgentState:
    """Execute a tool call returned by the LLM."""
    last = state["messages"][-1]
    result = tools.execute(last.tool_calls[0])   # validates schema + perms
    return {**state, "messages": state["messages"] + [result]}

def should_continue(state: AgentState) -> str:
    last = state["messages"][-1]
    if last.type == "ai" and not last.tool_calls:
        return END
    if state["steps"] >= state["max_steps"]:
        return END
    if hitl_gate.requires_approval(last):
        return "await_approval"
    return "dispatch"

graph = StateGraph(AgentState)
graph.add_node("reason", reason)
graph.add_node("dispatch", dispatch)
graph.add_conditional_edges("reason", should_continue)
graph.add_edge("dispatch", "reason")
graph.set_entry_point("reason")
agent = graph.compile()
Example 2 — HITL approval via webhook callback

# platform_sdk/hitl.py  (simplified illustrative implementation)
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable
import asyncio, uuid, httpx

@dataclass
class ApprovalRequest:
    request_id: str
    agent_name: str
    action: str
    payload: dict

_pending: dict[str, asyncio.Future] = {}

async def request_approval(action: str, payload: dict) -> bool:
    """Pause agent execution and wait for a human decision."""
    req_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    future: asyncio.Future = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()
    _pending[req_id] = future

    # Emit approval request to the platform webhook bus
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            "https://platform-internal/hitl/request",
            json=ApprovalRequest(req_id, "market-monitor", action, payload).__dict__,
        )

    # Block until callback arrives (timeout = 24 h)
    try:
        approved = await asyncio.wait_for(future, timeout=86400)
        return approved
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        del _pending[req_id]
        return False

# Called by the inbound webhook route when the approver submits a decision
def resolve_approval(request_id: str, approved: bool) -> None:
    if request_id in _pending:
        _pending[request_id].set_result(approved)

A financial services platform running dozens of agent workflows found that inconsistent HITL implementation was the leading cause of compliance findings. Some agents would call external payment APIs without any approval gate; others had approval gates that were never logged. After adopting a golden path:
Every agent that touched write APIs was automatically placed behind a HITL gate because the tool manifest flagged those tools as requiring approval.
All HITL events were written to the same immutable audit log format, making compliance reporting a single query rather than a multi-team aggregation exercise.
Token spend across 40 agents became visible through a unified dashboard within 48 hours of adoption — no bespoke instrumentation required.

A critical memory leak (unbounded context window growth) was caught by the memory manager's budget enforcement across all 40 agents simultaneously when the platform updated the golden path configuration.
allows a confused agent to burn thousands of tokens in a tight retry loop. Always configure the max_steps guard and wire it to the loop termination condition.
— granting an agent access to every tool in the registry when it only needs three. Use the
to list only the tools the agent genuinely requires. Principle of least privilege applies directly here.
Missing audit logs for HITL decisions
— logging only the request but not the approval outcome means you cannot answer 'who approved action X at time T' during an incident review. Log both request and response with actor identity.

— passing raw LLM output directly to a tool function without validating against the JSON Schema contract. The tool dispatcher in the golden path performs this validation; bypassing it to 'save a few milliseconds' removes an injection and type-safety defense.

— building agents that accumulate unlimited conversation history until the LLM context window is exhausted. The memory manager summarises and evicts proactively; configuring the token budget too high defeats this.
directly in application code prevents the platform from rolling out model upgrades. Always read the model from
Design state as a value type
— the state object should be immutable within each node; nodes return a delta or a new state, never mutate in place. This enables checkpointing and replay without side effects.
— mock the tool registry in unit tests and verify that the agent selects the correct tool for each scenario. Real API calls belong in integration tests, not unit tests.
— rate-limited APIs (search, finance data) need exponential backoff; idempotent write APIs need a deduplification key. Configure these in the tool manifest rather than inside the agent logic.
Use a lightweight model for summarisation
— the memory manager's summarisation step does not need the most capable model. A smaller, faster model cuts latency and token cost for this subtask.
Tie HITL timeout to business SLA
— a 24-hour approval window is fine for end-of-day reporting; a 5-minute window is required for time-sensitive automations. Make the timeout a parameter in
— conversation context belongs in the context window or Redis (working memory). Customer-facing results and audit records belong in a persistent store. Do not mix them.
Golden-path agents scale horizontally because the orchestrator (LangGraph/Temporal) holds no in-process state that prevents multiple replicas. Relevant tuning dimensions:
— a tight budget (60–80 k tokens) keeps per-request LLM cost low. The right ceiling depends on the longest realistic task; profile it before setting a permanent limit.
— LangGraph supports parallel tool dispatch in a single step. If the agent calls three independent APIs per turn, dispatch them concurrently with
— Redis working memory should expire within the expected max agent lifetime plus a safety margin. Stale working memory from crashed agents wastes memory and can mislead a restarted run.
— write to the vector store after the agent run completes, not during the loop. Async indexing prevents vector write latency from appearing on the critical path.
— when approval queues are deep, the approver interface must support batch decisions. Design the approval payload format so that reviewers can process 20 requests per page without context switching.

— LangChain's stateful graph engine. The canonical choice for DAG-based or cyclical single-agent flows. Supports in-memory and persistent (SQLite, Postgres) checkpointing. Versions >=0.2 support streaming token events.
— role-based multi-agent coordination. Each agent is a
member with a defined role, goal, and allowed tools. Suitable for scenarios where work is naturally divided between specialist agents (researcher, writer, fact-checker).

— durable execution for long-running agents. Workflow state is persisted after every activity; the agent survives pod restarts, LLM timeout retries, and deployment rollovers without replaying from scratch.
— vector stores for long-term semantic memory. Both support namespacing by agent ID so that one deployment serves many tenants.
— working memory (short-term, cross-step). Stores summarised conversation context, intermediate results, and deduplication keys.
— OTEL handles low-level span collection; Langfuse adds LLM-specific observability (prompt versions, token cost, tool call traces) without requiring a custom exporter.
TypedDict or BaseModel catches malformed LLM output before it propagates into tool calls.
New production agent apps on the platform
No built-in HITL, audit log, or memory budget enforcement
Teams must wire observability, HITL, and memory themselves
When the golden path scaffold is too constraining
Durable execution for long tasks; retry/timeout native
Higher operational cost; steeper learning curve
Agents that run for minutes to hours and cannot tolerate restarts
Less mature production story; fewer HITL primitives
Every tool in the registry has an associated permission scope (e.g.,
). The agent's ServiceAccount is granted only the scopes listed in its
. The tool dispatcher rejects calls to tools outside this set at runtime with a hard error — not a polite refusal from the LLM.
User-supplied content is placed in a clearly delimited block inside the system prompt (
) so the model has a structural boundary between trusted instructions and untrusted data.
Tool outputs returned by external APIs are treated as data, not instructions. They are never interpolated directly into the system prompt.
The tool dispatcher sanitises output that will be rendered to the user (strip control characters, truncate to configured max length) before appending it to the agent's message list.
— hard ceiling on loop iterations prevents runaway agents from consuming unbounded tokens or triggering unbounded tool calls.
— each tool call has a per-tool timeout configured in the manifest. A slow external API does not stall the agent indefinitely.
— the context budget guard ensures the agent never constructs a payload that exceeds the LLM's context window, preventing silent truncation errors.
— all approval events are appended to an immutable log store (append-only object storage or a write-once database table). Approved actions can be attributed to a named approver and timestamp.
The architecture diagram above shows how the platform scaffold, agent core, and external services connect. Key visual signals:
— synchronous request/response paths between the agent core and the LLM provider or tool registry.
— bidirectional memory read/write: the memory manager reads from context window and Redis, writes summarised state back.
— asynchronous observability events emitted from the agent core to the OTEL collector; these do not block the critical path.
— the approval pause path: agent emits an approval request and blocks until the human approver sends a callback.
— the orchestration layer wraps the agent core; the arrow from the agent core to the orchestration box represents the agent registering its compiled graph with the orchestrator runtime.
Think of the golden path as a

: a franchise owner receives a tested, proven operating model — store layout, supplier list, staff training, health and safety procedures. They do not redesign the kitchen; they operate the kitchen and focus on serving their local customers. A product team using the agent golden path operates the same way: the platform has already designed the kitchen (orchestration, memory, HITL, observability). The team's job is to write the
domain instructions and implement the tool-specific business logic.

The ReAct loop inside the agent is, mechanically, the same algorithm regardless of the task: reason about what information is missing, act by calling a tool to get that information, observe the result, repeat. The golden path scaffolds this loop with the production-quality concerns (budget, safety, audit) so the loop itself can be as simple as possible.
A golden path for agent apps encodes architectural decisions (orchestrator choice, memory layout, HITL wiring, observability) into a reusable scaffold generated by a single CLI command.
The ReAct loop, tool dispatch, memory manager, and HITL gate are the four structural components of every golden-path agent; each has a defined contract and can be tested independently.
suits long-running agents that must survive infrastructure restarts;
Tool permission scoping, prompt injection defenses, and output sanitisation are not optional hardening steps — they are pre-configured invariants of the golden path that must not be bypassed.
The memory manager's context budget enforcement prevents the most common production failure mode for long-running agents: silent context-window overflow.
Every HITL event must write to the audit log with actor identity and timestamp; approval without attribution is not an approval for compliance purposes.
Profile token cost and tool latency per agent before setting context budgets and concurrency limits; defaults in
are starting points, not final values.
